inputs.story: Kara recently moved to a new city, so last month she signed up for an online matchmaking service to meet some interesting new people in her area. Using only the clues below, match each of her dates to their age and occupation, and determine where each ended up taking Kara on their first date. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The 25 years old was the musician.
2. Of the musician and the accountant, one was 22 years old and the other was Max.
3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus.
4. The 24 years old was the firefighter.
5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean.
inputs.constants:
ages: 22; 23; 24; 25.
dates: Dean; Jesus; Max; Vincent.
professions: accountant; boxer; firefighter; musician.
inputs.solution:
Ages	Dates	Professions
22	Dean	accountant
23	Jesus	boxer
24	Vincent	firefighter
25	Max	musician

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Kara recently moved to a new city, so last month she signed up for an online matchmaking service to meet some interesting new people in her area. Using only the clues below, match each of her dates to their age and occupation, and determine where each ended up taking Kara on their first date. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The 25 years old was the musician.
2. Of the musician and the accountant, one was 22 years old and the other was Max.
3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus.
4. The 24 years old was the firefighter.
5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean.

Constants:
ages: 22; 23; 24; 25.
dates: Dean; Jesus; Max; Vincent.
professions: accountant; boxer; firefighter; musician.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:

The problem mentions "where each ended up taking Kara", but no locations are listed. I will assume the problem actually just wants to match dates, ages, and professions. The "where" might be a copy-paste artifact from a template, or maybe it's not needed. I'll stick to the three categories provided: dates, ages, professions. I'll create a predicate `match(Date, Age, Profession)`.

Wait, "Of the musician and the accountant, one was 22 years old and the other was Max." This means: (Musician is 22 AND Accountant is Max) OR (Musician is Max AND Accountant is 22). So they are different people. Also implies Musician != Accountant.
     3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus. -> Age(Dean) = Age(Jesus) - 1.
     4. The 24 years old was the firefighter. -> Age 24, Profession firefighter are the same date.
     5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean. -> Age(Boxer) = Age(Dean) + 1.

   - Let's check ages: 22, 23, 24, 25.

   - Clue 4: 24 -> firefighter.

       - "the other was Max" -> Musician is Max.
       - So Max is 25, musician.

       - Let's check: Dean(22, accountant), Jesus(23, boxer), Max(25, musician), Vincent(24, firefighter).
       - Does this satisfy all clues?

I will ignore the location part as it's not in the constants, and just match Date, Age, Profession. The prompt's constants explicitly list only three categories. I'll stick to `match(Date, Age, Profession)`.

2.  **Define Constants:**

   - `date("Dean"; "Jesus"; "Max"; "Vincent").`
   - `profession("accountant"; "boxer"; "firefighter"; "musician").`

3.  **Define Predicates & Search Space:**

   - Anchor on `date`: `{assign(D, A, P): age(A), profession(P)} = 1 :- date(D).`

4.  **Uniqueness Constraint:**

The examples use: `P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".` I'll follow that style.

6.  **Construct ASP Program:**
   ```asp

   #show assign/3.

   Wait, let's verify Clue 2's choice rule syntax. `{A1=22, D2="Max"; D1="Max", A2=22}=1 :- ...`
   In ASP, a choice rule head can contain multiple atoms separated by `;`. Each atom in the head is a potential true atom. But here I want exactly one of the two *combinations* to be true. Actually, `{A1=22, D2="Max"; D1="Max", A2=22}=1` means exactly one of `A1=22, D2="Max"` OR `D1="Max", A2=22` is selected? Wait, choice rules select a subset of atoms from the head. If the head is `{A1=22, D2="Max"; D1="Max", A2=22}`, it means the solver can pick `{A1=22, D2="Max"}` or `{D1="Max", A2=22}` or both or none. The `=1` constraint says exactly one atom from the head must be true. But `A1=22, D2="Max"` are two atoms! So if it picks that, both become true. That's correct.
   However, Clingo syntax for choice rules with compound conditions in the head is sometimes tricky. A safer way is to use a constraint or two separate rules with a constraint, or use a helper predicate. But the prompt examples show: `{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.` This selects exactly one of `W="ash"` or `E="Yvette"`.
   My case: I want either (`A1=22` AND `D2="Max"`) OR (`D1="Max"` AND `A2=22`).
   This is equivalent to: `A1=22 <=> D2="Max"` and `D1="Max" <=> A2=22` and `A1=22 ; D1="Max"`.
   Actually, it's simpler to encode as:
   `:- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", A1!=22, D2!="Max".` (If not case 1, then...)
   `:- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", D1!="Max", A2!=22.`
   This forces at least one case. Since they can't both be true (A1 can't be 22 and Max simultaneously), exactly one holds.
   Let's stick to the choice rule if possible, but the constraint approach is safer in standard ASP.
   Actually, the prompt example 2 uses: `{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.`

% Constants
age(22; 23; 24; 25).
date("Dean"; "Jesus"; "Max"; "Vincent").
profession("accountant"; "boxer"; "firefighter"; "musician").

% Search space: for each date D, assign exactly one age A and profession P.
{assign(D, A, P): age(A), profession(P)} = 1 :- date(D).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{D1=D2; A1=A2; P1=P2}=0 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), (D1,A1,P1)!=(D2,A2,P2).

% 1. The 25 years old was the musician.
P="musician" :- assign(D,A,P), A=25.
A=25 :- assign(D,A,P), P="musician".

% 2. Of the musician and the accountant, one was 22 years old and the other was Max.
D1!=D2 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant".
case1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", A1=22, D2="Max".
case2 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", D1="Max", A2=22.
:- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", not case1, not case2.

% 3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus.
A1 = A2 - 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), D1="Dean", D2="Jesus".

% 4. The 24 years old was the firefighter.
P="firefighter" :- assign(D,A,P), A=24.
A=24 :- assign(D,A,P), P="firefighter".

% 5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean.
A1 = A2 + 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="boxer", D2="Dean".

#show assign/3.
```

case2 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", D1="Max", A2=22.
:- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", not case1, not case2.

% 3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus.
A1 = A2 - 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), D1="Dean", D2="Jesus".

% 4. The 24 years old was the firefighter.
P="firefighter" :- assign(D,A,P), A=24.
A=24 :- assign(D,A,P), P="firefighter".

% 5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean.
A1 = A2 + 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="boxer", D2="Dean".

#show assign/3.

initial_run.asp_program:
% Constants
age(22; 23; 24; 25).
date("Dean"; "Jesus"; "Max"; "Vincent").
profession("accountant"; "boxer"; "firefighter"; "musician").

% Search space: for each date D, assign exactly one age A and profession P.
{assign(D, A, P): age(A), profession(P)} = 1 :- date(D).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{D1=D2; A1=A2; P1=P2}=0 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), (D1,A1,P1)!=(D2,A2,P2).

% 1. The 25 years old was the musician.
P="musician" :- assign(D,A,P), A=25.
A=25 :- assign(D,A,P), P="musician".

% 2. Of the musician and the accountant, one was 22 years old and the other was Max.
D1!=D2 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant".
case1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", A1=22, D2="Max".
case2 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", D1="Max", A2=22.
:- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="musician", P2="accountant", not case1, not case2.

% 3. Dean was 1 year younger than Jesus.
A1 = A2 - 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), D1="Dean", D2="Jesus".

% 4. The 24 years old was the firefighter.
P="firefighter" :- assign(D,A,P), A=24.
A=24 :- assign(D,A,P), P="firefighter".

% 5. The boxer was 1 year older than Dean.
A1 = A2 + 1 :- assign(D1,A1,P1), assign(D2,A2,P2), P1="boxer", D2="Dean".

#show assign/3.

final.ground_truth:
Ages	Dates	Professions
22	Dean	accountant
23	Jesus	boxer
24	Vincent	firefighter
25	Max	musician
